text: The following events were reported: Flash Flood, Flood. These occurred near station USW00012844, approximately 15.9085 km away, between 2020-10-24 00:00:00 and 2020-10-29 18:00:00. The flood was caused by Heavy Rain. Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. No significant property or crop damage was reported. Episode Narrative: Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. As an upper level trough traversed aloft, additional deep moisture overspread the South Florida ahead of an approaching frontal boundary. This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas. Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. Event Narrative: A member of the broadcast media reported flooding via social media at the intersection of 6th Ave South and Congress Ave in Lake Worth. Two right lanes are seen flooded with water rising over the curb and is about 2 inches deep on the sidewalk. Cars are moving very slowly through the roadway. Report from MPING of water overflowing resulting in property flooding. A member of the public reported flooding of roadways from heavy rainfall in Boynton Beach along Indian Wells Blvd. Emergency managers and town officials reported that significant flooding persisted in the Sea Pines neighborhood in Lantana from heavy rain late on October 24th and early on October 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets continued to be impassable for most of this time frame, and many residents were stranded. Local assessments indicated that high-water marks were observed just underneath window sills in a few homes, with a number of homes having water intrusion. Over 1 million gallons of water were pumped out of the neighborhood. The public reported that flooded roadways continue in the Delray Beach area, including Lake Blvd and Military Trail vicinity due to heavy rainfall over the weekend. Portions of the street are impassable due to standing water up to curbs. Significant flooding occurred in the Sea Pines neighborhood of Lantana resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Numerous calls were received from residents stranded in their homes, some who had water in their homes up to their knees. Streets were impassable across the entire neighborhood. Flood waters lingered for several days in the area (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Significant flooding occurred in the Lake Boynton Estates area of Boynton Beach,  resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Episode Narrative: Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. As an upper level trough traversed aloft, additional deep moisture overspread the South Florida ahead of an approaching frontal boundary. This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas. Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. Event Narrative: A member of the broadcast media reported flooding via social media at the intersection of 6th Ave South and Congress Ave in Lake Worth. Two right lanes are seen flooded with water rising over the curb and is about 2 inches deep on the sidewalk. Cars are moving very slowly through the roadway. Report from MPING of water overflowing resulting in property flooding. A member of the public reported flooding of roadways from heavy rainfall in Boynton Beach along Indian Wells Blvd. Emergency managers and town officials reported that significant flooding persisted in the Sea Pines neighborhood in Lantana from heavy rain late on October 24th and early on October 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets continued to be impassable for most of this time frame, and many residents were stranded. Local assessments indicated that high-water marks were observed just underneath window sills in a few homes, with a number of homes having water intrusion. Over 1 million gallons of water were pumped out of the neighborhood. The public reported that flooded roadways continue in the Delray Beach area, including Lake Blvd and Military Trail vicinity due to heavy rainfall over the weekend. Portions of the street are impassable due to standing water up to curbs. Significant flooding occurred in the Sea Pines neighborhood of Lantana resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Numerous calls were received from residents stranded in their homes, some who had water in their homes up to their knees. Streets were impassable across the entire neighborhood. Flood waters lingered for several days in the area (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Significant flooding occurred in the Lake Boynton Estates area of Boynton Beach,  resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Episode Narrative: Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. As an upper level trough traversed aloft, additional deep moisture overspread the South Florida ahead of an approaching frontal boundary. This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas. Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. Event Narrative: A member of the broadcast media reported flooding via social media at the intersection of 6th Ave South and Congress Ave in Lake Worth. Two right lanes are seen flooded with water rising over the curb and is about 2 inches deep on the sidewalk. Cars are moving very slowly through the roadway. Report from MPING of water overflowing resulting in property flooding. A member of the public reported flooding of roadways from heavy rainfall in Boynton Beach along Indian Wells Blvd. Emergency managers and town officials reported that significant flooding persisted in the Sea Pines neighborhood in Lantana from heavy rain late on October 24th and early on October 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets continued to be impassable for most of this time frame, and many residents were stranded. Local assessments indicated that high-water marks were observed just underneath window sills in a few homes, with a number of homes having water intrusion. Over 1 million gallons of water were pumped out of the neighborhood. The public reported that flooded roadways continue in the Delray Beach area, including Lake Blvd and Military Trail vicinity due to heavy rainfall over the weekend. Portions of the street are impassable due to standing water up to curbs. Significant flooding occurred in the Sea Pines neighborhood of Lantana resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Numerous calls were received from residents stranded in their homes, some who had water in their homes up to their knees. Streets were impassable across the entire neighborhood. Flood waters lingered for several days in the area (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Significant flooding occurred in the Lake Boynton Estates area of Boynton Beach,  resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Episode Narrative: Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. As an upper level trough traversed aloft, additional deep moisture overspread the South Florida ahead of an approaching frontal boundary. This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas. Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. Event Narrative: A member of the broadcast media reported flooding via social media at the intersection of 6th Ave South and Congress Ave in Lake Worth. Two right lanes are seen flooded with water rising over the curb and is about 2 inches deep on the sidewalk. Cars are moving very slowly through the roadway. Report from MPING of water overflowing resulting in property flooding. A member of the public reported flooding of roadways from heavy rainfall in Boynton Beach along Indian Wells Blvd. Emergency managers and town officials reported that significant flooding persisted in the Sea Pines neighborhood in Lantana from heavy rain late on October 24th and early on October 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets continued to be impassable for most of this time frame, and many residents were stranded. Local assessments indicated that high-water marks were observed just underneath window sills in a few homes, with a number of homes having water intrusion. Over 1 million gallons of water were pumped out of the neighborhood. The public reported that flooded roadways continue in the Delray Beach area, including Lake Blvd and Military Trail vicinity due to heavy rainfall over the weekend. Portions of the street are impassable due to standing water up to curbs. Significant flooding occurred in the Sea Pines neighborhood of Lantana resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Numerous calls were received from residents stranded in their homes, some who had water in their homes up to their knees. Streets were impassable across the entire neighborhood. Flood waters lingered for several days in the area (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Significant flooding occurred in the Lake Boynton Estates area of Boynton Beach,  resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Episode Narrative: Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. As an upper level trough traversed aloft, additional deep moisture overspread the South Florida ahead of an approaching frontal boundary. This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas. Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. Event Narrative: A member of the broadcast media reported flooding via social media at the intersection of 6th Ave South and Congress Ave in Lake Worth. Two right lanes are seen flooded with water rising over the curb and is about 2 inches deep on the sidewalk. Cars are moving very slowly through the roadway. Report from MPING of water overflowing resulting in property flooding. A member of the public reported flooding of roadways from heavy rainfall in Boynton Beach along Indian Wells Blvd. Emergency managers and town officials reported that significant flooding persisted in the Sea Pines neighborhood in Lantana from heavy rain late on October 24th and early on October 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets continued to be impassable for most of this time frame, and many residents were stranded. Local assessments indicated that high-water marks were observed just underneath window sills in a few homes, with a number of homes having water intrusion. Over 1 million gallons of water were pumped out of the neighborhood. The public reported that flooded roadways continue in the Delray Beach area, including Lake Blvd and Military Trail vicinity due to heavy rainfall over the weekend. Portions of the street are impassable due to standing water up to curbs. Significant flooding occurred in the Sea Pines neighborhood of Lantana resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Numerous calls were received from residents stranded in their homes, some who had water in their homes up to their knees. Streets were impassable across the entire neighborhood. Flood waters lingered for several days in the area (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Significant flooding occurred in the Lake Boynton Estates area of Boynton Beach,  resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.

Episode Narrative: Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. As an upper level trough traversed aloft, additional deep moisture overspread the South Florida ahead of an approaching frontal boundary. This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas. Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. Event Narrative: A member of the broadcast media reported flooding via social media at the intersection of 6th Ave South and Congress Ave in Lake Worth. Two right lanes are seen flooded with water rising over the curb and is about 2 inches deep on the sidewalk. Cars are moving very slowly through the roadway. Report from MPING of water overflowing resulting in property flooding. A member of the public reported flooding of roadways from heavy rainfall in Boynton Beach along Indian Wells Blvd. Emergency managers and town officials reported that significant flooding persisted in the Sea Pines neighborhood in Lantana from heavy rain late on October 24th and early on October 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets continued to be impassable for most of this time frame, and many residents were stranded. Local assessments indicated that high-water marks were observed just underneath window sills in a few homes, with a number of homes having water intrusion. Over 1 million gallons of water were pumped out of the neighborhood. The public reported that flooded roadways continue in the Delray Beach area, including Lake Blvd and Military Trail vicinity due to heavy rainfall over the weekend. Portions of the street are impassable due to standing water up to curbs. Significant flooding occurred in the Sea Pines neighborhood of Lantana resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Numerous calls were received from residents stranded in their homes, some who had water in their homes up to their knees. Streets were impassable across the entire neighborhood. Flood waters lingered for several days in the area (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Significant flooding occurred in the Lake Boynton Estates area of Boynton Beach,  resulting from a combination of heavy rainfall of the previous days plus 4-5 inches of rain on the night and early morning of October 24th and 25th. Two to three homes had water intrusion, and water was about 2 feet deep on streets. Four families in the area requested to be evacuated. Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood). Heavy rainfall in the Boynton Beach area led to significant street flooding in the Seacrest and Chapel Hill neighborhoods. 8-10 inches of rain fell in the area over the previous several days, with 4-5 inches on the evening of October 24th and early morning of October 25th. Water was approximately 2 feet deep on some roadways, and a few lift stations were submerged. City of Lake Worth Beach reported 3 homes with visible water marks on garage doors, however no major structural impacts were noted. Standing water from heavy rainfall on October 24th and 25th lingered in the Fairfield Drive area of Mangonia Park for several days. Main impacts were impassable streets, and one business along Fairfield Drive was affected, although no water intrusion into structures was reported. Standing water from days of heavy rainfall affected fields and farms in unincorporated westernmost areas of Boynton Beach west of State Road 7 and bordering on the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge. Horses were up to their knees in standing water for a few days and required removal from the affected properties. Flooding lingered in the Lake Boynton Estates neighborhood for over 24 hours following the heavy rainfall of October 24th and 25th (see Flash Flood entry in this episode for more information on the causative event). Streets were impassable in the area, along with 2-3 homes with water intrusion which required evacuations.
